module: Organize a Fabric lakehouse using medallion

questions.question: How is the gold layer typically modeled for business use?

options.A: As a flat raw file dump
options.B: As a star schema with facts and dimensions

questions.explanation: The gold layer is where data is modeled for business use, typically as a star schema with fact tables and dimension tables, ready for downstream consumers.

questions.question: Which four workspace roles apply to everything in a Fabric workspace?

options.A: Owner, Editor, Reader, Guest
options.B: Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer

questions.explanation: Workspace roles are Admin, Member, Contributor, and Viewer, and they apply to everything in that workspace. Item permissions are more targeted than workspace roles.

questions.question: A small early-stage team wants the simplest medallion setup to manage. Which lakehouse structuring option best fits their needs?

options.A: Separate workspaces per layer
options.B: Separate lakehouses per layer
options.C: A single lakehouse with bronze, silver, and gold schemas
options.D: A separate Data Warehouse for each layer

questions.explanation: A single lakehouse with schemas is simpler to manage and good for smaller teams or early-stage projects, while still letting you group tables by layer and set per-layer permissions.

questions.question: An organization operates under strict regulatory requirements that demand the strongest possible isolation between medallion layers, including separate capacity. Which structuring approach should they choose?

options.A: Single lakehouse with schemas
options.B: Separate lakehouses in one workspace

questions.explanation: Separate workspaces provide the strongest isolation and are required for regulatory or compliance scenarios; each layer gets its own capacity, role assignments, and ownership boundary.

questions.question: Source data for the bronze layer already resides in Amazon S3 and the team wants it to stay in sync automatically without writing any ingestion code. What should they use?

options.A: A pipeline copy activity scheduled hourly
options.B: A OneLake shortcut to the S3 location
options.D: A materialized lake view over S3

questions.explanation: When the source is already in cloud storage such as Amazon S3, a OneLake shortcut references it in place without copying and keeps bronze in sync automatically, with no pipeline or ingestion code.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to load data into bronze from an on-premises source that is NOT in cloud storage. Which approach is appropriate?

options.A: Only a OneLake shortcut
options.B: Pipelines, dataflows, or notebooks

questions.explanation: For sources that are not already in cloud storage, you use pipelines, dataflows, or notebooks to load data into the bronze layer. Shortcuts are only for sources already in cloud storage.

questions.question: A team needs to apply complex custom calculations and API calls during a silver-layer transformation on a very large dataset. Which Fabric tool is the right choice?

options.A: Dataflows (Gen2)
options.B: Notebooks

questions.explanation: Notebooks give full control using Python or SQL and are the right choice for large datasets, complex logic, custom calculations, API calls, or complex joins that a dataflow can't handle.

questions.question: A team wants to define a silver transformation in SQL and have Fabric automatically keep the resulting table current as new bronze data arrives, updating only changed rows. Which feature fits?

options.A: A regular SQL view
options.B: A materialized lake view
options.C: A Dataflow Gen2 on a schedule

questions.explanation: A materialized lake view lets you define a transformation in SQL; Fabric saves the results as a real table and, leveraging Delta change tracking, updates only the rows that changed when new bronze data arrives, with no scheduling needed.

questions.question: A transformation logic requires Python and cannot be expressed purely in SQL. What is the recommended alternative to a materialized lake view?

options.A: A regular SQL view
options.B: A notebook

questions.explanation: The tradeoff of a materialized lake view is that the transformation must be expressible as SQL. If you need Python logic, use a notebook instead.

questions.question: Analysts who only know SQL need direct access to query the modeled gold layer tables without anyone building a separate query layer. What should they use?

options.A: The SQL analytics endpoint
options.B: A OneLake shortcut

questions.explanation: The SQL analytics endpoint gives any team member with SQL knowledge direct read-only access to the gold layer, allowing T-SQL queries, saved functions, views, and SQL security without moving data.

questions.question: A Power BI report author needs to build reports on the gold layer. According to the guidance, what should the report connect to?

options.A: The raw Delta tables directly
options.B: The bronze schema

questions.explanation: For Power BI users, the gold layer is surfaced through a semantic model. Power BI reports connect to the semantic model, not directly to the raw Delta tables.

questions.question: Multiple teams share one workspace, but a gold-layer consumer must be able to query gold tables while being blocked from seeing bronze and silver, without creating separate workspaces. What should be configured?

options.A: Workspace Viewer role for everyone
options.B: OneLake data access roles scoped to specific tables or folders

questions.explanation: OneLake data access roles give granular control within a single lakehouse without separate workspaces; you can scope a role to specific tables or folders so a gold consumer can query gold but not see bronze or silver.

questions.question: A data science team wants a flat, wide table optimized for feature engineering, while finance wants pre-aggregated summaries, both from the same refined data. What does the module say is possible?

options.A: Only one gold model can exist per lakehouse
options.B: You can have multiple gold layers, same silver underneath, each modeled for a team's needs
options.C: Each team must maintain its own silver layer
options.D: Feature engineering must use bronze data directly

questions.explanation: You can have multiple gold layers serving different audiences using the same silver data underneath, each gold layer modeled differently (for example, a wide table for data science and pre-aggregated summaries for finance).

questions.question: An administrator wants to configure granular table-level security within a single lakehouse. Where do they start in the Fabric UI?

options.A: Open the lakehouse and select Manage OneLake security
options.B: Open the workspace settings and assign Admin role
options.C: Open Power BI and create a semantic model

questions.explanation: To configure OneLake security, you open the lakehouse and select Manage OneLake security, then create a role, define its scope, and assign members.

questions.question: A team needs to promote their medallion workspace from development to test to production in a controlled sequence, comparing environments to catch differences before they reach production data. Which capability supports this?

options.A: OneLake shortcuts
options.B: Deployment pipelines

questions.explanation: Deployment pipelines let you promote a medallion workspace from development to test to production in a controlled sequence, with the ability to compare environments and catch differences before they reach production.

questions.question: A bad deployment corrupted a transformation in the silver layer. The team had connected the workspace to Git. What is the fastest path to recovery described in the content?

options.A: Re-ingest from the source system manually
options.B: Revert to the previous commit
options.D: Switch the semantic model to Import mode

questions.explanation: With Git integration, if a transformation change breaks the silver layer, you can revert to the previous commit. Teams can also work in branches and merge through pull requests.

questions.question: An architect wants clearer separation between layers and the ability to apply different permissions per layer, but does not need separate capacities or compliance-grade isolation. Which option best matches?

options.A: Single lakehouse with schemas
options.B: Separate lakehouses

questions.explanation: Separate lakehouses are best for clearer separation and applying different permissions per layer, sitting between a single lakehouse (simplicity) and separate workspaces (strongest isolation).

questions.question: An analyst complains that a Power BI report on the gold layer requires a manual refresh to see new data. The semantic model uses Direct Lake mode. What is the expected behavior?

options.A: Direct Lake requires nightly imports
options.B: Reports always show current data without a separate refresh step
options.C: Direct Lake only works on bronze data
options.D: A scheduled dataflow must refresh the model

questions.explanation: Direct Lake reads directly from the Delta files in OneLake instead of importing a copy, so reports always show current data without a separate refresh step.

questions.question: Why does a materialized lake view avoid reprocessing the entire silver table each time new bronze data arrives?

options.A: It caches query results in Power BI
options.B: Delta format tracks every change, so Fabric updates only the new rows
options.C: It reruns the query on every read like a regular view
options.D: It copies all data to a separate warehouse

questions.explanation: Because all Fabric tables are stored in Delta format which tracks every change, Fabric checks the change log and updates only the new rows in silver, rather than reprocessing the whole table.

questions.question: A team wants to grant a colleague access to one specific lakehouse without giving access to the entire workspace. What mechanism should they use?

options.A: Workspace Admin role
options.B: Item permissions

questions.explanation: Item permissions are more targeted than workspace roles; you can share a specific lakehouse with a colleague without giving them access to the whole workspace.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a regular SQL view and a materialized lake view in Fabric?

options.A: A regular view stores results as a table; a materialized view recomputes each query
options.B: A regular view reruns its query on every read; a materialized lake view saves results as a real table
options.D: A materialized lake view can only read from gold

questions.explanation: A regular view reruns its query every time someone queries it, whereas a materialized lake view saves the results as a real table and keeps it current automatically using Delta change tracking.

questions.question: Which statement best reflects how the medallion layers map to audiences in the content?

options.A: Bronze for business users, silver for engineers, gold for analysts
options.B: Bronze for data engineers, silver for analysts and data scientists, gold for business users
options.C: All layers serve business users equally
options.D: Gold for data engineers, bronze for business users

questions.explanation: Bronze is the raw landing zone for data engineers, silver is a clean integrated dataset for analysts and data scientists, and gold is a dimensional model for business users.

questions.question: An organization argues the three-layer pattern is too rigid and wants a domain-specific layer for one team beyond gold, plus a landing zone before bronze. According to the content, is this acceptable?

options.A: No, exactly three layers are mandatory
options.B: Yes, the names and number of layers are flexible as long as each has a clear purpose and audience
options.C: Only if separate workspaces are used
options.D: Only the SQL endpoint allows this

questions.explanation: The three-layer pattern is a starting point, not a strict rule. You can add a landing zone before bronze or a domain-specific layer beyond gold; the names and number of layers are flexible as long as each has a clear purpose and audience.

questions.question: Typical silver-layer activities include which of the following?

options.A: Storing data exactly as received with no changes
options.B: Combining sources, removing nulls, deduplicating, standardizing formats

questions.explanation: Silver-layer transformations focus on quality and consistency: standardizing formats, removing nulls, deduplicating records, and joining data from multiple bronze sources into a unified dataset.

questions.question: Which tools are used to build the gold layer, and what coordinates them?

options.A: Only the SQL analytics endpoint, coordinated by Power BI
options.B: Dataflows, notebooks, or materialized lake views, coordinated by pipelines
options.D: Only semantic models, coordinated by deployment pipelines

questions.explanation: You build the gold layer using the same tools as silver (dataflows, notebooks, or materialized lake views), coordinated by pipelines.

questions.question: To create a Power BI semantic model over the gold layer, what step does the content describe?

options.A: Enable Direct Lake on bronze tables
options.B: Select New semantic model from the lakehouse and choose which gold tables to include
options.C: Run a CREATE SEMANTIC MODEL SQL statement
options.D: Add a OneLake shortcut to Power BI

questions.explanation: To create a semantic model, you select New semantic model from the lakehouse and choose which gold layer tables to include.

questions.question: Contoso Retail collects raw sales transactions, clickstream events, and product catalog updates in varied formats arriving at different rates. They plan a Fabric medallion lakehouse. Sales data already lives in ADLS Gen2 and must stay in sync without ingestion code. The silver layer needs a SQL-defined sales table that standardizes region casing, casts order dates, computes total_amount, drops rows with missing order IDs, and auto-updates as new bronze rows arrive. The finance team needs pre-aggregated gold summaries while data science needs a wide flat table, both from the same refined data. Analysts query gold via T-SQL and business users use Power BI reports that must always show current data. Finally, gold consumers in a shared workspace must be blocked from bronze and silver. Which combination of choices correctly satisfies ALL of these requirements?

options.A: OneLake shortcut for ADLS Gen2 sales; a materialized lake view for the silver sales table; multiple gold layers (wide for data science, summaries for finance); SQL analytics endpoint for analysts; a Direct Lake semantic model for Power BI; OneLake data access roles scoping gold consumers away from bronze/silver
options.B: A scheduled pipeline copy for ADLS Gen2 sales; a regular SQL view for silver; a single gold layer reused by both teams; Import-mode Power BI; workspace Admin role for all gold consumers
options.C: OneLake shortcut for sales; a notebook-only silver because SQL cannot standardize text; one gold layer; DirectQuery semantic model; separate workspaces are the only way to block bronze access
options.D: Dataflow Gen2 to copy ADLS Gen2 sales nightly; materialized lake view in gold only; bronze queried directly by Power BI; DefaultReader left unchanged for all users

questions.explanation: A OneLake shortcut syncs cloud-storage sales without ingestion code; a materialized lake view defines the SQL transformation and auto-updates only changed rows via Delta tracking; multiple gold layers can serve finance and data science from the same silver; the SQL analytics endpoint serves analysts; a Direct Lake semantic model keeps Power BI reports current without manual refresh; and OneLake data access roles scope gold consumers away from bronze/silver within a shared workspace. The other options misuse tools (regular views recompute, Import/DirectQuery don't match Direct Lake's always-current behavior, leaving DefaultReader unchanged exposes all data, and SQL can standardize text so a notebook is not required).

questions.question:
Consider this Fabric SQL statement:

CREATE MATERIALIZED LAKE VIEW silver.sales AS
SELECT order_id, customer_id,
       UPPER(TRIM(region)) AS region,
       CAST(order_date AS DATE) AS order_date,
       unit_price * quantity AS total_amount
FROM bronze.sales
WHERE order_id IS NOT NULL;

Which statement correctly describes its behavior?

options.A: It creates a regular view that reruns the query on every read
options.B: It saves results as a real silver table and, as new bronze rows arrive, Fabric updates only the changed rows automatically
options.C: It copies all bronze data into a Power BI semantic model
options.D: It requires a scheduled pipeline trigger to refresh

questions.explanation: A materialized lake view saves results as a real table. Leveraging Delta change tracking, when new rows arrive in bronze Fabric updates only the new rows in silver automatically, with no schedule or trigger needed.

questions.question: Given input bronze.sales rows where region is '  east ' and '  East ', what value does the expression UPPER(TRIM(region)) AS region produce for both?

options.A: '  east '
options.B: 'East'

questions.explanation: TRIM removes the surrounding spaces and UPPER converts to uppercase, so both '  east ' and '  East ' standardize to 'EAST', enforcing consistent formatting in the silver layer.

questions.question:
A PySpark notebook builds a silver table from bronze:

df = spark.read.table("bronze.orders")
silver = (df.dropna(subset=["order_id"])
            .dropDuplicates(["order_id"]))
silver.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver.orders")

Which silver-layer responsibilities does this code implement?

options.A: Star-schema modeling and aggregation for business users
options.B: Removing nulls and deduplicating records to produce a clean dataset
options.C: Raw landing of unmodified source data
options.D: Creating a Power BI semantic model

questions.explanation: dropna(subset=['order_id']) removes rows with null order IDs and dropDuplicates(['order_id']) deduplicates records. These are classic silver-layer data quality activities; the result is written back as a Delta table.

questions.question:
A notebook builds a gold star-schema fact table:

gold_fact = (spark.read.table("silver.sales")
  .groupBy("customer_id", "order_date")
  .agg(F.sum("total_amount").alias("daily_total")))
gold_fact.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("gold.fact_sales")

Which medallion principle does this best illustrate?

options.A: Bronze stores raw data unmodified
options.B: Gold aggregates refined silver data to the granularity needed for reporting
options.C: Shortcuts keep data in sync with the source

questions.explanation: The gold layer is shaped for business consumption, often aggregated to the granularity needed for reporting. Here silver.sales is aggregated to a daily total per customer and written as a gold fact table, consistent with star-schema modeling.

options.A: Object 1 saves results as a table; Object 2 recomputes each time
options.B: Object 1 recomputes the query on every read; Object 2 serves a saved table kept current incrementally
options.D: Both save results and never update

questions.explanation: A regular view (Object 1) reruns its query every time it is queried. A materialized lake view (Object 2) saves results as a real table and updates only changed rows incrementally via Delta, so repeated queries read the saved table rather than recomputing.

questions.question:
A Spark SQL statement attempts to enforce silver-layer integration by joining two bronze sources:

CREATE MATERIALIZED LAKE VIEW silver.customer_orders AS
SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id, c.customer_name, o.total_amount
FROM bronze.orders o
JOIN bronze.customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id;

Which silver-layer goal does this primarily achieve?

options.A: Storing raw unmodified data
options.B: Combining and integrating data from multiple bronze sources into a unified dataset
options.C: Serving a Power BI semantic model in Direct Lake

questions.explanation: Joining data from multiple bronze sources into a unified dataset is a core silver-layer activity, producing a consistent integrated dataset that analysts and data scientists can use directly.

questions.question: A developer writes a gold transformation in SQL but the logic also needs to call an external REST API to enrich records mid-transformation. Which Fabric tool should implement this, and why?

options.A: A materialized lake view, because it supports arbitrary Python
options.B: A notebook, because API calls and custom logic exceed what a SQL-expressible materialized view can do
options.C: A regular SQL view, because views can call APIs
options.D: A OneLake shortcut, because it transforms data in place

questions.explanation: A materialized lake view's transformation must be expressible as SQL. API calls and custom logic require a notebook, which gives full control using Python or SQL for complex scenarios.

questions.question: An administrator scripts OneLake security so that only gold tables are visible to a consumer role. Conceptually, the role is created, scoped to the gold tables/folders, and members assigned, while DefaultReader is restricted. What outcome does this achieve?

options.A: Gold consumers can read gold but cannot see bronze or silver, within a single shared lakehouse
options.B: Gold consumers gain workspace Admin rights
options.C: All ReadAll users automatically see every layer
options.D: It forces every layer into a separate workspace

questions.explanation: OneLake data access roles can be scoped to specific tables or folders so a gold consumer can query gold tables but cannot see bronze or silver, all within a single lakehouse. Restricting the built-in DefaultReader role prevents the default all-data access.
